findings.summary: Several BaseChannel and Tool subclass docstrings restate the class name without adding insight.
evidence: DiscordChannel docstring: 'Discord channel using Gateway websocket.' — restates the class name and import path
SlackChannel docstring: 'Slack channel using Socket Mode.' — same pattern
ReadFileTool docstring: 'Tool to read file contents.' — the name 'ReadFileTool' already conveys this
ExecTool docstring: 'Tool to execute shell commands.' — identical pattern across all 12 Tool subclasses
findings.suggestion: Remove one-line docstrings that restate the class name. Reserve docstrings for non-obvious behavior, caveats, or usage notes.

findings.summary: BaseChannel methods have multi-line Args docstrings on parameters whose types and names are already self-documenting.
evidence: is_allowed() has a 5-line docstring for a 1-param method 'sender_id: The sender's identifier.' — the type hint str already says this
_handle_message() has a 10-line Args section documenting 'content: Message text content', 'media: Optional list of media URLs' which are self-evident from type hints
send() has 4-line docstring for single-param method: 'msg: The message to send' — adds nothing beyond the type hint
findings.suggestion: Trim docstrings on base class methods to a single purpose line. The Args sections restate type hints; remove them.

findings.summary: config/loader.py uses bare print() for error output instead of the project's loguru logger.
evidence: Line 33: print(f'Warning: Failed to load config from {path}: {e}')
Line 34: print('Using default configuration.')
Every other nanobot-src module uses 'from loguru import logger' for structured logging
findings.suggestion: Replace the two print() calls with logger.warning() to maintain consistent logging.

findings.summary: nanobot/utils/helpers.py has multi-line Args/Returns docstrings on trivial 2-5 line utility functions.
evidence: get_workspace_path() has a 7-line docstring (Args + Returns) for a 4-line function body
parse_session_key() has a 6-line docstring (Args + Returns) for a 3-line function body
findings.suggestion: Reduce multi-line docstrings to single-line summaries for trivial utility functions.

findings.summary: The _split_message() function is independently implemented in telegram.py and discord.py with nearly identical logic.
evidence: telegram.py:81 defines _split_message(content, max_len=4000) with line-break-preferring chunking
discord.py:23 defines _split_message(content, max_len=MAX_MESSAGE_LEN) with identical chunking logic
Both functions: find last newline, fall back to space, fall back to hard cut — same algorithm
findings.suggestion: Extract a shared split_message(content, max_len) function into channels/base.py or a channels/utils.py module.

findings.summary: AgentLoop.__init__ accepts 14 parameters, making it a wide parameter bag despite the from_config classmethod.
evidence: AgentLoop.__init__ has 14 named params
The from_config classmethod centralizes unpacking but doesn't reduce the init surface
Parameters like brave_api_key, temperature, max_tokens, memory_window could be grouped into a config/defaults object
findings.suggestion: Consider accepting an AgentDefaults config object for the LLM-related knobs to reduce the init signature.

findings.summary: All 12 Tool subclasses repeat the same @property boilerplate for name, description, and parameters instead of using class attributes.
evidence: base.py defines name, description, parameters as @property @abstractmethod
WebSearchTool and WebFetchTool already use class attributes directly without @property
ReadFileTool, WriteFileTool, EditFileTool, ListDirTool, ExecTool, MessageTool, SpawnTool, CronTool all implement 3 @property methods each
findings.suggestion: Change the base Tool class to accept name/description/parameters as regular class attributes.

findings.summary: CronService manually serializes/deserializes CronJob dataclasses to/from JSON dicts instead of using Pydantic.
evidence: _load_store() manually constructs CronJob from nested dict access over 30 lines
_save_store() manually serializes back to nested dicts over 30 lines
The config module already uses Pydantic models with model_validate/model_dump
findings.suggestion: Convert CronJob and related types to Pydantic models and use model_validate/model_dump for serialization.

findings.summary: Backend uses stdlib logging while nanobot-src uses loguru — two logging frameworks with no interop bridge.
evidence: All 6 backend/app modules use 'import logging; logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)'
All 20+ nanobot-src modules use 'from loguru import logger'
No loguru intercept handler or stdlib-to-loguru bridge configured
findings.suggestion: Choose one logging strategy or add a loguru intercept handler in the backend's startup to unify output.

findings.summary: Tool subclasses use two incompatible patterns for defining name/description/parameters.
evidence: WebSearchTool and WebFetchTool define them as class-level attributes
ReadFileTool, WriteFileTool, EditFileTool, ListDirTool, ExecTool, etc. define them as @property methods
Both patterns work but the inconsistency is surprising
findings.suggestion: Standardize all tool subclasses to use class-level attributes (matching WebSearchTool/WebFetchTool).

findings.summary: The channels_status CLI command hardcodes 9 channel display blocks instead of iterating over channel config.
evidence: cli/commands.py lines 611-688: channels_status() has 9 sequential blocks
channels/manager.py lines 37-50: _CHANNEL_DEFS is a data-driven list
Adding a new channel requires updating both
findings.suggestion: Make channels_status() data-driven by iterating over channel metadata.

findings.summary: Package __init__.py files have inconsistent content: some have docstrings, one is empty.
evidence: channels/__init__.py has docstring, agent/__init__.py has docstring
backend/app/routes/__init__.py is empty (no docstring)
None export any symbols
findings.suggestion: Pick one convention and apply consistently.

findings.summary: nanobot/server.py defines _make_provider() that is a pure pass-through wrapper to registry.make_provider().
evidence: nanobot/server.py lines 37-46: def _make_provider(config): ... from nanobot.providers.registry import make_provider; return make_provider(config)
The function adds no policy, error handling, or translation beyond what make_provider already provides
findings.suggestion: Replace the _make_provider wrapper with a direct import and call of make_provider from the registry.

findings.summary: nanobot/server.py uses module-level mutable globals (agent_loop, _init_error) modified via `global` statements.
evidence: Line 32: agent_loop = None (module-level mutable)
Line 34: _init_error = None (module-level mutable)
Line 78: global agent_loop, _init_error -- modified from init_agent()
handle_client() reads agent_loop at line 165 without synchronization
findings.suggestion: Encapsulate agent_loop, _ready, and _init_error in a small Server or AppState class.

findings.summary: backend/app/main.py imports _recreate_container from app.routes.node_status, creating a dependency from app root into route layer.
evidence: backend/app/main.py line 29: from app.routes.node_status import _recreate_container
This creates a dependency from the application bootstrap layer into the route layer
findings.suggestion: Move _recreate_container into a shared module like app/container_ops.py.

findings.summary: The project has both nanobot/ (Dockerfile + server.py) and nanobot-src/ (actual package), creating confusing boundaries.
evidence: nanobot/ contains only Dockerfile and server.py -- deployment artifact
nanobot-src/ contains the actual nanobot Python package
nanobot/server.py imports from the nanobot-src package
findings.suggestion: Rename the nanobot/ deployment directory to deploy/ or nanobot-deploy/.

findings.summary: Config._match_provider() reimplements model-matching logic that already exists in registry.find_by_model().
evidence: schema.py _match_provider() and registry.find_by_model() both iterate PROVIDERS, match by keyword, normalize prefixes
Both normalize with model_lower.replace('-', '_'), split on '/', check normalized_prefix == spec.name
findings.suggestion: Have Config._match_provider() delegate to registry.find_by_model().

findings.summary: LiteLLMProvider._setup_env() mutates os.environ and litellm globals during construction.
evidence: Line 51: litellm.api_base = api_base -- mutates litellm global
Lines 69-71: os.environ[spec.env_key] = api_key -- mutates process environment
Line 54: litellm.suppress_debug_info = True -- mutates litellm global
findings.suggestion: Pass api_key and api_base directly via kwargs to litellm.acompletion() instead of mutating global state.

findings.summary: backend requires pydantic>=2.0 while nanobot-src requires >=2.12.0 -- ranges diverge.
evidence: backend/pyproject.toml: pydantic>=2.0
nanobot-src/pyproject.toml: pydantic>=2.12.0,<3.0.0
findings.suggestion: Align backend pydantic requirement to >=2.12.0,<3.0.0.

findings.summary: backend requires websockets>=14.0 vs nanobot-src's >=16.0,<17.0 -- overlap only at 16.x.
evidence: backend/pyproject.toml: websockets>=14.0
nanobot-src/pyproject.toml: websockets>=16.0,<17.0
findings.suggestion: Tighten backend websockets requirement to >=16.0,<17.0.

findings.summary: socksio is declared in telegram optional group but never directly imported.
evidence: pyproject.toml: socksio>=1.0.0,<2.0.0 in [telegram] extras
grep for 'import socksio' returns zero matches
findings.suggestion: Verify if socksio is needed as transitive dep; if so add comment, if not remove.

findings.summary: custom_provider.py imports from openai but openai is not listed in dependencies.
evidence: custom_provider.py: from openai import AsyncOpenAI
openai not in pyproject.toml dependencies
findings.suggestion: Add openai>=1.0.0 as explicit dependency or restructure CustomProvider.

findings.summary: backend/pyproject.toml specifies only lower bounds for all dependencies.
evidence: All 7 dependencies use only >= lower bounds
nanobot-src correctly pins upper bounds
findings.suggestion: Add upper-bound version constraints to backend dependencies.

findings.summary: nodes.py (798 LOC) handles 7 distinct domain concerns in one file.
evidence: 28 route handler functions across 7 domain concerns in a single file
Network topology has no relationship to workspace file CRUD
21 disconnected function clusters per static analysis
findings.suggestion: Split into focused route modules: node_crud, node_container, node_workspace, node_network, node_children.

findings.summary: Identity name-sync pattern duplicated in nodes.py, chat.py, and main.py.
evidence: All three use json.loads -> name comparison -> json.dumps(indent=2) -> write_workspace_file sequence
findings.suggestion: Extract a shared sync_identity_name() function in helpers.py.

findings.summary: Network topology query implemented independently in nodes.py and chat.py with near-identical logic.
evidence: Both use identical SQL patterns for edge queries and sibling computation
Only difference is chat.py omits edge_type in node_summary
findings.suggestion: Extract shared get_network_topology() in helpers.py.

findings.summary: AgentLoop.__init__ accepts 14 parameters.
evidence: 14 named params, many related config values should be grouped
from_config classmethod exists but raw init remains wide
findings.suggestion: Group LLM-related params into AgentDefaults dataclass.

findings.summary: CronService manual JSON serialization is fragile and duplicative.
evidence: _load_store/_save_store manually pack/unpack dicts over 60+ lines
Project already uses Pydantic extensively
findings.suggestion: Convert CronJob types to Pydantic BaseModel subclasses.

findings.summary: chat_relay WebSocket handler has 3-4 levels of nested closures.
evidence: 146-line function with nested async closures
nonlocal frontend_connected flag creates implicit coupling
findings.suggestion: Extract closures as module-level async functions.

findings.summary: LLM providers return errors as content strings instead of raising.
evidence: Both catch all exceptions and return LLMResponse(content='Error...')
finish_reason='error' is set but never checked by any caller
findings.suggestion: Raise a custom LLMError or check finish_reason == 'error' in the agent loop.

findings.summary: Tools return 'Error: ...' strings but registry also catches exceptions — dual error path.
evidence: Registry checks result.startswith('Error') — fragile string-prefix convention
WebFetchTool returns JSON-encoded errors breaking the pattern
findings.suggestion: Define ToolError or ToolResult type for consistent error detection.

findings.summary: docker_ops.py uses three different error strategies inconsistently.
evidence: get_* return None/strings, write_* silently log, run_container_command propagates
No clear rule for which strategy applies
findings.suggestion: Get functions return Optional, mutation functions raise on failure.

findings.summary: Some routes raise HTTPException on container errors, others return 200 with error state hidden in config.
evidence: create_node/clone_node return 200 with error in config
restart_node/rebuild_node raise HTTPException 500
findings.suggestion: Choose one strategy: always raise or always return with distinct status.

findings.summary: nanobot/server.py uses print() instead of logging/loguru.
evidence: 5+ print() calls in server.py
Backend uses logging, nanobot-src uses loguru — server.py uses neither
findings.suggestion: Replace print() with loguru logger.

findings.summary: Multiple channels silently swallow exceptions with bare except:pass.
evidence: matrix.py has 4 silent suppression instances
server.py on_progress wraps websocket.send in except Exception: pass
findings.suggestion: Add logger.debug() to all bare except:pass blocks.

findings.summary: WebFetchTool returns JSON-encoded errors while all other tools return plain strings.
evidence: WebFetchTool returns json.dumps({'error': ...})
All other tools return f'Error: ...' strings
Registry startswith('Error') check won't match JSON errors
findings.suggestion: Change WebFetchTool to use plain string format matching other tools.
